## What is artificial intelligence?

Artificial intelligence is the study of building systems that perform tasks normally
requiring human intelligence. The field has gone through several waves of progress
since Alan Turing posed the question "Can machines think?" in 1950.

Three paradigm shifts stand out. Rule-based expert systems dominated the 1970s and
1980s. Statistical learning methods gained ground in the 1990s and 2000s. Deep
learning, powered by large datasets and GPU hardware, triggered the current wave
after 2012.

## The three machine learning paradigms

Machine learning is the subfield of AI where systems learn patterns from data rather
than following hand-coded rules.

**Supervised learning** uses labelled examples. Given input-output pairs, the model
learns a mapping from inputs to outputs. Regression predicts a continuous value;
classification predicts a discrete category.

**Unsupervised learning** works with unlabelled data. The model discovers structure
such as clusters, principal components, or density estimates without knowing the
correct answer in advance.

**Reinforcement learning** trains an agent to take actions in an environment and
maximize cumulative reward. It is the paradigm behind game-playing systems and
robotic control.

::: {.teacher-only}
Emphasize that most of this course focuses on supervised learning (regression and
classification) with a detour into unsupervised methods. Reinforcement learning is
mentioned for context only.
:::

## AI success stories

**Computer vision** was transformed by deep learning. The ImageNet Large Scale Visual
Recognition Challenge saw error rates drop from 26% in 2011 to below 3% by 2017,
surpassing average human performance. All top entries after 2012 used deep
convolutional neural networks.

**Natural language processing** advanced rapidly with the introduction of transformer
architectures. Modern language models can summarize, translate, and generate text at
a level that was unreachable a decade ago.

**Game intelligence** demonstrated that reinforcement learning agents can master
complex strategy games. AlphaGo defeated the world champion in Go in 2016, a
milestone once considered decades away.

## Building a machine learning system

Every supervised learning system requires four ingredients:

1. A **dataset** of input-output examples.
2. A **model** that defines the family of functions we search over.
3. An **objective function** (loss function) that measures how far predictions are
   from the truth.
4. An **optimization algorithm** that adjusts the model parameters to minimize the
   loss.

The rest of this course fills in each ingredient, starting with linear regression
and gradient descent.
